annotations.label: Parameter (general)

anchor.snippet: is any characteristic that can help in defining or classifying a particular system

annotations.note: An informal general-language definition of 'parameter', not a mathematical statement formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parameters in a model

anchor.snippet: the values that describe the system are called parameters

annotations.note: Informal modeling terminology, not a formalizable mathematical object.

annotations.label: Parametrization

anchor.snippet: choosing a convenient set of parameters is called parametrization

annotations.note: A general meta-level notion of choosing parameters, with no single formal counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Position on a sphere

anchor.snippet: if one were considering the movement of an object on the surface of a sphere

annotations.note: An illustrative physical example rather than a stated theorem or definition.

annotations.label: Parameter in a function definition

anchor.snippet: parameters are not listed among the arguments that the function takes

annotations.note: An informal distinction between parameters and arguments, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: General quadratic function

anchor.snippet: one could define a general quadratic function by declaring

annotations.note: Mathlib has quadratic discriminant API (discrim) but no 'general quadratic function' parametric-family definition matching this illustrative example.

annotations.label: Base-b logarithm

anchor.snippet: one may define the base- b logarithm by the formula

annotations.note: `Real.logb b x = log x / log b` is exactly the base-b logarithm defined by this formula.

annotations.label: Falling factorial power

anchor.snippet: the notation for the falling factorial power

annotations.note: `Nat.descFactorial` (and the polynomial `descPochhammer`) directly formalize the falling factorial power.

annotations.label: Parametric family

anchor.snippet: consider all functions with certain parameters as parametric family

annotations.note: A general informal notion of a parametric family with no dedicated Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Car speed and gas pedal

anchor.snippet: the speed of the car, depends on the independent variable, the position of the gas pedal

annotations.note: An everyday illustrative example, not a mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Parametric equaliser

anchor.snippet: A parametric equaliser is an audio filter that allows the frequency of maximum cut or boost to be set by one control

annotations.note: An audio-engineering example, not formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Relationship y = ax^2

anchor.snippet: If asked to imagine the graph of the relationship y = ax 2

annotations.note: An illustrative remark about a as a parameter, not a stated theorem.

annotations.label: Income, wage, and hours worked

anchor.snippet: In calculating income based on wage and hours worked

annotations.note: An economics example, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Variables vs parameters (Bard)

anchor.snippet: the distinction between variables and parameters was described by Bard as follows

annotations.note: An informal philosophical distinction quoted from Bard, not formalizable.

annotations.label: Parameter of a curve

anchor.snippet: a curve can be described as the image of a function whose argument, typically called the parameter , lies in a real interval

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes parametrized curves via `Path` (continuous maps from an interval), but not this exact informal 'parameter of a curve' definition.

annotations.label: Unit circle parametrization

anchor.snippet: the unit circle can be specified in the following two ways

annotations.note: `circleMap 0 1 θ = exp(θ·I)` gives the trigonometric parametrization of the unit circle, generalized to arbitrary center and radius.

annotations.label: Integral depending on a parameter

anchor.snippet: integrals dependent on a parameter are often considered

annotations.note: Mathlib has substantial parametric-integral infrastructure (differentiation under the integral sign) but no single definition naming an 'integral depending on a parameter'.

annotations.label: Frequentist vs Bayesian parameters

anchor.snippet: whereas in Bayesian estimation they are treated as random variables

annotations.note: A methodological distinction in statistics not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Statistic vs parameter

anchor.snippet: estimand refers to populations, where the samples are taken from

annotations.note: Statistical terminology (statistic vs population parameter) without a Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Sample mean as estimator

anchor.snippet: the sample mean (estimator)

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks a developed estimator/sample-mean statistics framework, so this is not formalized.

annotations.label: Non-parametric statistics

anchor.snippet: one speaks of non-parametric statistics as opposed to the parametric statistics just described

annotations.note: A statistics terminology distinction not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Family of distributions by parameters

anchor.snippet: distinguished from each other by the values of a finite number of parameters

annotations.note: Mathlib defines specific parametrized distributions but not the general informal notion of a finitely-parametrized distribution family.

annotations.label: Poisson distribution

anchor.snippet: a Poisson distribution with mean value

annotations.note: `poissonPMF r` formalizes the Poisson distribution with rate/mean parameter r.

annotations.label: Radioactive sample emissions

anchor.snippet: suppose we have a radioactive sample that emits, on average, five particles every ten minutes

annotations.note: A physical worked example of the Poisson distribution, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Normal distribution parameters

anchor.snippet: the normal distribution , which has as parameters the mean μ and the variance σ²

annotations.note: `gaussianReal μ v` is the normal distribution parametrized exactly by mean μ and variance v.

annotations.label: Parameterized distribution

anchor.snippet: we have a parameterized distribution

annotations.note: The generic notion of a parameterized distribution has no single Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Moments or cumulants as parameters

anchor.snippet: It is possible to use the sequence of moments

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `moment`, `mgf`, and `cgf` but does not prove that the moment sequence characterizes a distribution; cumulants are not defined.

annotations.label: Formal vs actual parameter

anchor.snippet: more formally as a formal parameter and an actual parameter

annotations.note: A programming-language concept, not a mathematical statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Formal parameter x

anchor.snippet: x is the formal parameter (the parameter ) of the defined function

annotations.note: A programming example, not formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Actual parameter 3

anchor.snippet: 3 is the actual parameter (the argument ) for evaluation by the defined function

annotations.note: A programming example, not formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parameters as weights

anchor.snippet: Parameters in a model are the weight of the various probabilities

annotations.note: An AI/machine-learning usage of 'parameter', not a Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Parameter in engineering

anchor.snippet: the term parameter sometimes loosely refers to an individual measured item

annotations.note: An informal engineering usage, not formalizable.

annotations.label: Properties vs parameters

anchor.snippet: parameters are those combinations of the properties which suffice to determine the response of the system

annotations.note: An informal engineering distinction without a Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Parameter in environmental science

anchor.snippet: a parameter is used to describe a discrete chemical or microbiological entity that can be assigned a value

annotations.note: A domain-specific terminology definition, not formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parameter in linguistics

anchor.snippet: almost exclusively used to denote a binary switch in a Universal Grammar

annotations.note: A linguistics concept, not a mathematical statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parameters vs variables in logic

anchor.snippet: Parameters locally defined within the predicate are called variables

annotations.note: An informal logical distinction between parameters and variables, not formalized as such in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parameter in music theory

anchor.snippet: a parameter denotes an element which may be manipulated (composed), separately from the other elements

annotations.note: A music-theory usage of 'parameter', not formalizable in Mathlib.
